region_of_origin: Mexico and Central America (introduced to Southeast Asia via Spanish Manila Galleon trade route, 16th–17th century; now widely cultivated in Malaysia, Indonesia, Philippines, Thailand; Singapore imports primarily from Malaysia and Thailand)
food_subgroup: Root and tuber vegetables — raw jicama flesh (Pachyrhizus erosus); very low calorie (38 kcal/100g); high inulin prebiotic fibre (~5g/100g)

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00JC used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 code for raw peeled jicama flesh confirmed in published FoodEx2 catalogue — code retained from input record with closest_parent designation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for jicama root food product in available SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

serving.description: 100g raw peeled jicama flesh (reference portion); approximately 80g as jicama component in one popiah (fresh spring roll)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw peeled jicama is approximately 100g (reference portion) or ~80g as the jicama filling component in one Singapore popiah fresh spring roll.

nutrient_source.title: Jicama, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #11603
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy Foods Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 11603 is the canonical entry for raw jicama (Pachyrhizus erosus). All per-100g nutrient values are taken directly from this entry. FAO/INFOODS SEA Regional Database consulted as secondary corroboration for Southeast Asian context.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database entry for jicama/yam bean
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~36 for jicama (Pachyrhizus erosus) is consistent with published values in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). The low GI reflects that approximately 70% of jicama carbohydrate is inulin (non-digestible fructooligosaccharide) which does not raise blood glucose; only the ~30% digestible fraction (sucrose/starch) contributes to glycaemic response. GL = 1.4 per 100g serving (GI 36 × 3.9g available carbohydrate / 100). Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (4mg/100g); low potassium (150mg/100g) suitable for CKD/potassium-restricted patients; inulin prebiotic supports gut-derived SCFA production with secondary cardiovascular benefits
hypertension.guidance: Jicama is highly suitable for hypertension management: sodium content is negligible at 4mg/100g, and its unusually low potassium (150mg/100g) makes it one of the few vegetables safe for patients on potassium restriction (CKD, ACE inhibitor-induced hyperkalaemia); inulin-derived SCFAs (butyrate, propionate) have demonstrated modest antihypertensive effects via GPR41/43 receptor signalling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI 36 (low), GL 1.4 (negligible); ~5g/100g inulin stimulates GLP-1 secretion via gut microbiome SCFA pathway — the same incretin mechanism targeted by semaglutide and liraglutide
type2_diabetes.guidance: Jicama is an excellent food choice for Type 2 Diabetes: GI ~36 and GL ~1.4 per 100g produce minimal postprandial glucose excursion; the high inulin content (~5g/100g) is fermented by colonic Bifidobacterium to short-chain fatty acids (butyrate), which stimulate L-cell GLP-1 secretion — the endogenous incretin pathway that is pharmacologically targeted by GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide, dulaglutide) — providing a food-based prebiotic mechanism for improved glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, zero trans fat; high dietary fibre (4.9g/100g including ~5g inulin); very low energy density (38 kcal/100g); inulin prebiotic supports favourable lipid profile via SCFA-mediated hepatic cholesterol synthesis inhibition
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Jicama presents a very low cardiovascular risk profile: it contains zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and zero trans fat per 100g; its high inulin fibre content supports LDL cholesterol reduction via propionate-mediated inhibition of hepatic HMG-CoA reductase activity, and its very low caloric density (38 kcal/100g) supports weight management — a key modifiable CVD risk factor.

ckd_chronic_kidney_disease.key_factor: Exceptionally low potassium (150mg/100g) — among the lowest of all vegetables; low phosphorus (18mg/100g)
ckd_chronic_kidney_disease.guidance: Jicama is one of the safest vegetables for CKD patients on potassium restriction, with only 150mg potassium and 18mg phosphorus per 100g; it can be consumed without the potassium-leaching preparation steps required for most root vegetables.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low inflammatory profile; inulin prebiotic supports gut microbiome diversity relevant to RA pathogenesis; vitamin C (20mg/100g) supports collagen synthesis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Jicama's inulin prebiotic content supports gut microbiome diversity, which is increasingly recognised as relevant to RA pathogenesis and biologic drug efficacy; vitamin C (20.2mg/100g) supports collagen synthesis and may modestly reduce oxidative stress in inflamed joints.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jicama (Pachyrhizus erosus), known in Singapore and Malaysia as bangkuang or sengkuang, and in Thailand as man kaeo (มันแกว), is a crisp, mildly sweet root vegetable with white starchy flesh. It is consumed raw after peeling away the inedible brown papery skin. The texture is firm and juicy, similar to a water chestnut or apple, with a neutral-sweet flavour that absorbs dressings and sauces well. In Singapore hawker culture, bangkuang is an essential ingredient in popiah (fresh spring roll filling, typically braised), yusheng (raw fish salad for Chinese New Year), and rojak (spicy fruit-and-vegetable salad). Nutritionally, raw peeled jicama is very low calorie at 38 kcal per 100g, with 0.7g protein, 0.1g fat, 8.8g carbohydrate, and 4.9g dietary fibre. A critical nutritional feature is its high inulin content (~5g/100g) — inulin is a prebiotic fructooligosaccharide that selectively stimulates Bifidobacterium growth in the colon, producing short-chain fatty acids (butyrate, propionate, acetate) that stimulate GLP-1 secretion from intestinal L-cells — the same incretin pathway targeted by GLP-1 receptor agonist medications (semaglutide, liraglutide). GI is approximately 36 (low), with a negligible glycaemic load of ~1.4 per 100g. Sodium is negligible at 4mg/100g. Potassium is unusually low at 150mg/100g, making jicama one of the few root vegetables safe for CKD patients on potassium restriction. CRITICAL FOOD SAFETY: Only the white inner flesh is edible — the leaves, seeds, and skin of Pachyrhizus erosus contain rotenone, a mitochondrial complex I inhibitor that is toxic to humans and animals. Source: USDA SR Legacy FDC #11603.

confidence_reason: Nutrient data sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC #11603 (exact match for raw jicama, Pachyrhizus erosus), an approved primary source with full macro and micronutrient coverage. GI value of ~36 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. All values confirmed at confidence 1.0 in input record. FAO/INFOODS SEA consulted as secondary corroboration.
notes: ZERO-VALUE JUSTIFICATIONS: cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — jicama is a plant food and inherently contains no cholesterol (naturally zero). vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero — jicama is a plant food and inherently contains no vitamin B12 (naturally zero). vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per USDA FDC #11603 (confirmed zero). saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FDC #11603 (confirmed zero). trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FDC #11603 (confirmed zero). NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for raw jicama not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC #11603 as of 2026-04-14. water_g set to -1: moisture content not extracted from USDA FDC #11603 for this record; USDA reports approximately 86.6g water/100g for raw jicama but this field was not populated in the input record and is noted here for completeness. FOOD SAFETY NOTE: CRITICAL — only the white inner flesh of Pachyrhizus erosus is edible. The leaves, seeds, and skin contain rotenone (a mitochondrial complex I inhibitor / insecticide), which is toxic to humans, fish, and animals. This safety warning must be communicated whenever jicama is discussed. ONTOLOGY NOTE: FoodEx2 code A00JC retained as closest_parent designation from input record; exact FoodEx2 code for raw peeled jicama flesh not independently confirmed in published FoodEx2 v2 catalogue — flagged for expert review. INULIN NOTE: Inulin content ~5g/100g is cited from FAO/INFOODS SEA and published literature (Van Loo et al.); USDA FDC #11603 does not separately report inulin as a distinct field but includes it within total dietary fibre (4.9g/100g).
